coredata.dc:title: Mannitol capped magnetic dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction of polar drugs sparfloxacin and orbifloxacin from milk and water samples followed by selective fluorescence sensing using boron-doped carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Hassan Refat H.
Hassan, Ahmed I.
Hassan, Yasser F.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, mannitol capped magnetic nanoparticles were fabricated for dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction of sparfloxacin (SPX) and orbifloxacin (ORX) from milk and natural water samples. The synthesized magnetic sorbent is readily dispersed in the water and easily isolated magnetically from the medium after loaded with the cited analytes. The effect of main adsorption experimental parameters were studied and optimized. The small amount of the sorbent (15 mg) is applicable for the preconcentration in a reasonable time (15 min). Additionally, the adsorption characterization fits the pseudo-second order kinetic and Langmuir isotherm model with a high determination coefficient. Magnetic nanoparticles were modified with mannitol polar groups to adsorb polar drugs (SPX and ORX) from complicated matrices via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. After micro-extraction, SPX and ORX were analyzed using boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) as a fluorescence nanoprobe, as the extracted SPX and ORX favor the fluorescence of B-CQDs via hydrogen bonding and electron donor-acceptor association (charge-transfer complex). The synthesized nanomaterials were characterized by different techniques such as Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), and UV/Vis spectroscopy. Under the optimum conditions, the fluorimetric method exhibited the increase of fluorescence intensity in the range of 1.5 × 10−9–1.3 × 10−7 M and 1.2 × 10−9–1.1 × 10−7 M with limits of detection (LODs) of 5.0 × 10−10 and 4.0 × 10−10 M for SPX and ORX, respectively. The proposed dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction based fluorimetric method was applied for the determination of SPX and ORX in milk and natural water samples with satisfactory results.

head.ce:title: Mannitol capped magnetic dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction of polar drugs sparfloxacin and orbifloxacin from milk and water samples followed by selective fluorescence sensing using boron-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al Azhar University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, mannitol capped magnetic nanoparticles were fabricated for dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction of sparfloxacin (SPX) and orbifloxacin (ORX) from milk and natural water samples. The synthesized magnetic sorbent is readily dispersed in the water and easily isolated magnetically from the medium after loaded with the cited analytes. The effect of main adsorption experimental parameters were studied and optimized. The small amount of the sorbent (15 mg) is applicable for the preconcentration in a reasonable time (15 min). Additionally, the adsorption characterization fits the pseudo-second order kinetic and Langmuir isotherm model with a high determination coefficient. Magnetic nanoparticles were modified with mannitol polar groups to adsorb polar drugs (SPX and ORX) from complicated matrices via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. After micro-extraction, SPX and ORX were analyzed using boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) as a fluorescence nanoprobe, as the extracted SPX and ORX favor the fluorescence of B-CQDs via hydrogen bonding and electron donor-acceptor association (charge-transfer complex). The synthesized nanomaterials were characterized by different techniques such as Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), and UV/Vis spectroscopy. Under the optimum conditions, the fluorimetric method exhibited the increase of fluorescence intensity in the range of 1.5 × 10−9–1.3 × 10−7 M and 1.2 × 10−9–1.1 × 10−7 M with limits of detection (LODs) of 5.0 × 10−10 and 4.0 × 10−10 M for SPX and ORX, respectively. The proposed dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction based fluorimetric method was applied for the determination of SPX and ORX in milk and natural water samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluoroquinolones (FQs) are widely applied as a broad spectrum antibacterial in veterinary and human medicines that can be accumulated in livestock products and water resources and ultimately lead to negative health impacts among exposed food consumers as it potentiate allergic reactions, hepatotoxicity, and gastrointestinal upset [1]. Furthermore, the impact of bacterial resistance and the increasing loss of antibiotic effectiveness is a significant challenge for both animal health and public health safety; hence proper measures must be taken to quantify these residues to limit the emergence of antimicrobial resistance [2]. SPX and its analog ORX belong to the third generation fluoroquinolones that were prohibited in food-producing animals by Federal law depending on a finding by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) that the extra-label use presents a risk to the public health because of alterations on intestinal microflora and hypersensitivity reactions, and it could increase the level of drug-resistant zoonotic pathogens [3]. The European Union has established the maximum residue limits (MRLs) for ORX and SPX should be set at 30 and 50 ng mL−1, respectively (Commission Regulation (EU) No. 37/2010 of 22 December) [2].

However, few reports about analytical quantitation of SPX and ORX are available in the literature including chromatography [4–7], electrochemistry [8,9], immune-assay [10], microbiological assay [11], and optical sensing [12,13]. Chromatographic methods have several disadvantages such as the need for highly skilled persons, solvent consumption, expensive instrumentation, and exhaustive extraction steps. Despite the prominent sensitivity of electrochemical methods, the low stability of nanomaterials and poor reproducibility are the main drawbacks that restricted their clinical applications [14]. Optical sensing methods have several advantages such as good reproducibility, fast response, simplicity, and ease of visualization [15].

The fluorimetric methods for SPX analysis lack sensitivity and selectivity while no fluorometric methods were found in the literature for analysis of ORX. Fluorescence “Turn On” nanoprobes are less likely than fluorescence quenching sensors to give false-positive signals and are therefore generally preferred [16].

Dispersive magnetic dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction (dMSPE) is an inexpensive, simple, and fast approach that is used for extraction, clean up and concentration of various analytes in real samples with high enrichment factors and low sample consumption [17,18]. Various nanomaterials were used to preconcentrate analytes from their matrix [19–21], one of the nanomaterial sorbents is magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs), which can alternate traditional solid-phase extraction SPE sorbents due to their small particle size, high specific surface area, high sorption capacity, and high selectivity related to analytes in addition to ease isolation of magnetic sorbents from the sample solution by virtue of an external magnetic field without the need for centrifugation, filtration or gravitation separation [22–24]. The passivation of MNPs surface with a sugar moiety such as mannitol provides a good colloidal constancy and responsive potential for pharmaceutical extraction applicability [25].

Photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as one of the nanomaterial family members have drawn much attention in diverse areas in the past decade due to its unique optical, electric, and biocompatible properties [26,27]. Doping of heteroatoms into CQDs skeleton has been recognized to attune the conduction/valence band position of doped CQDs leading to modulated functions. The inclusion of boron into the CQDs skeleton has intrinsic practical importance since it can treat the defects, impurities, electrical, and luminescent tendencies of CQDs [28]. It is noteworthy to mention that no report was found in the literature utilizing mannitol functionalized Fe3O4 NPs as a nano-sorbent and B-CQDs as an optical nanosensor for enhancement the selectivity and sensitivity of the detection. The fabricated mannitol functionalized Fe3O4 NPs can efficiently extract polar compounds from complicated matrices via formation of multiple hydrogen bonds. While, the presence of fluorine groups in the cited drugs (SPX and ORX) make them able to selectively bind B-CQDs, facilitating charge transfer and so the improvement of SPX and ORX detection amongst the other members.

Inspired by the above circumstances, we designed a multifunctional sensing probe based on B-CQDs as an artful approach for detection of SPX and ORX via fluorescence enhancement. Fluorescent response of the B-CQDS is augmented by SPX and ORX derived from potent hydrogen-bond interaction and donor-acceptor association complex. It should be pointed out that this is the first time to utilize fluorescent B-CQDs for detection of SPX and ORX residues in real sample matrices coupled with dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction. This method is more advantageous than other traditional ones as it is environmentally friendly, highly selective, and sensitive.

Boric acid 99%, FeCl3·6H2O, glycine, cysteine, and glucose were purchased from Acros Organics (USA). FeCl2·4H2O, mannitol, CaCl2, and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were bought from Isochem specification industry, Egypt. Starch, sucrose, urea, ascorbic acid, and CoCl2 were provided by Lab-Chem fine chemicals, Egypt.

Pharmaceutical grade ciprofloxacin hydrochloride (CPX) and norfloxacin (NRX) were supplied by Kahira Pharmaceuticals & Chemical Industries Company, Egypt. Ofloxacin powder (OFX) was kindly supplied by the Egyptian International Pharmaceutical Industries Company, EIPICO, Egypt, Moxifloxacin hydrochloride (MOX) was obtained from EVA Pharm. & Chem. Ind. Company, Egypt. Levofloxacin hydrochloride (LVX) was obtained from Memphis Pharmaceuticals & Chemical Industries Co. Egypt. Lomefloxacin hydrochloride (LMX) was provided by Sigma Pharmaceutical Industries, Egypt. Sparfloxacin (SPX) and Pefloxacin (PFX) were supplied by Global Napi pharmaceuticals Company, Egypt.
Orbifloxacin (ORX) was obtained from Merck animal health; USA. Flumequine (FLQ) and enrofloxacin (ENX) were purchased from Evpco, Egypt.
Bovine raw milk was purchased commercially from a supermarket (Assuit, Egypt) while Ibrahimia conduit water (Assuit, Egypt) was utilized as a natural water sample.

A stock solution (1.0 × 10−3 M) of SPX and ORX by dissolving 39.2 mg in 100 mL 0.05 NaOH and 39.5 mg in 100 mL doubly distilled water (DDW), respectively. Britton–Robinson (B.R., 0.04 M) buffer was used to control the pH [29].

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of B-CQDS was recorded by a UV-1601PC UV–VIS spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were carried out on a SCINCO FluoroMate (FS-2, Korea) fluorometer. The surface morphology images of synthesized nanomaterials were obtained by a JEOL-100CX II tungsten-filament 100 kV transmission electron microscope TEM (USA) at 200 kV and scanning electron microscope SEM, (Hitachi, Japan). The functional groups on the synthesized nanomaterials were measured with a Nicolet™iS™10 FTIR spectrometer in the wavenumber range 400–4000 cm−1 via the KBr pellet method (Germany). The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) technique was utilized to perform elemental micro-analysis using the OXFORD AZtec instrument. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) measurements were investigated via Philips/1710 diffractometer, Particle size distribution measurements were recorded by Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) technique using Zeta-sizer Nano ZS (Malvern, UK). Magnetic characterization of the magnetic nanoparticles was processed by The WEISTRON Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (Model: VSM1100). Applying ultra-sound energy to agitate particles to dissolve, preconcentrate, or extract the sample by MTI Corporation, Ultrasonic Cleaner (USA). Other instruments including a V-1 plus vortex mixer, BOECO, (Germany), an 80-2 15/20 mL Electronic Lab Centrifuge Machine, (China), a Box10114/B97WD electric oven, (UK), a JSWB-T Series digital display temperature control water Bath, (Korea), and An Adwa AD11P pH-meter, Romania.

Magnetic nanoparticles were selected due to several advantages such as ease of preparation, reducing the procedure time by eliminating the centrifuge stage, and easily functionalized [25]. In addition, polar groups modified MNPs were synthesized to selectively bind polar drugs such as SPX and ORX via hydrogen bond formation and electrostatic interactions. Then, the MNPs were isolated from the aqueous medium by virtue of an external magnet and then the target analytes were desorbed for further application. Compared to traditional SPE, sample preconcentration is significantly easier by means of the dSPME whereby applying an external magnetic field without additional steps that could cause loss of analytes [30]. Secondly, the passivation of the MNPs surface with several multifunctional moieties offers proper coating and protection strategies. The chemical structure of mannitol as a six-carbon resistant sugar alcohol conducts a potential stabilizing and protection rather than the other modifiers; also the highly negatively charged hydroxyl environment promotes electrostatic binding of positively charged analytes [25]. Boron-doped carbon dots (B-CQDs) were used to quantify extracted drugs via enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of B-CQDs. Boron is the nearest neighbor of the carbon in the periodic table. By replacing one C by B in the carbon skeleton, the total electron density in the framework did not change which leads to several important nano-electronic enforcement by virtue of atomic radii similarity of B to C [28]. In addition, the replacement of C atoms by B ultimately promotes the local chemical reactivity which enhances the binding energy inducing an electron-donor-acceptor complex between the FQs molecules and the B-CQDs which it is remarkably not only modulate the chemical binding configuration, but also the physicochemical properties in comparison to undoped CQDs [31].

MMNPs were synthesized by one-pot co-precipitation of Fe2+ and Fe3+ salts at the molar ratio of 1:2 in ammoniacal medium followed by in-situ passivation of mannitol as in [25] with some melioration. Briefly, 0.994 g of FeCl2·4H2O and 2.703 g of FeCl3·6H2O were dissolved in 50 mL DDW under vigorous stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, 1.0 g of mannitol was dissolved in the solution and kept for another 20 min under stirring. 10 mL (25%) NH3 solution was then added drop-wise to the stirring mixture. After complete adding NH3, heat the above reaction mixture at 70 °C for 30 min to ensure complete passivation of MNPs with mannitol. The magnetic nanoparticles were isolated using an external magnetic field and thoroughly washed DDW three times with the help of the magnet, then inserted into the oven under drying conditions of 50 °C for 3 h. Finally, the dried magnetic nanoparticles were easily re-dispersible in water ( Scheme 1). For comparison, unmodified MNPs were also fabricated following the same synthetic procedure without mannitol passivation.

The B-CQDs were formulated via a hydrothermal route [32]. Briefly, 0.5 g glucose and 0.3 g boric acid were dissolved in 50 mL of DDW through sonication for 30 min. Then the solution was heated at 300 °C for 4 h in an electric oven. After cooling down, the output mixture was centrifuged at 1800g for 30 min and the solid product was collected, washed with DDW and ethanol three times, and dried. B-CQDs (100.0 mg) were dispersed in 100.0 mL DDW where a homogenized bright yellow colloid was obtained, and then the obtained colloid was further subjected to sonication for 30 min and filtration to remove the large particles (Scheme 1). For comparison, undoped CQDs were also synthesized under the same synthetic procedure without boric acid incorporation.

ORX and ORX are used prominently in lactating animals, which may cause residues in milk, so commercial sterilized bovine milk samples were bought from a local supermarket in El-Qusya City (Assiut, Egypt) and took as a sample for testing the real applicability of the proposed method. Preliminary assays exhibited the absence of SPX and ORX residues. 5.0 mL fresh milk samples were spiked with 1.0 µM standard solution of SPX and ORX and stored at −4 °C until analysis. Deproteination of milk samples was performed by adding 5.0 mL of acetonitrile and centrifuged at 1800g for 15 min; the clear supernatant was subjected to an extraction experiment [33]. Also, the presence of ORX and SPX in wastewater from swine farms can introduce antibiotics into surface waters through agricultural runoff and may persist within environmental waters because of their sorption properties, favoring accumulation in sewage sludge, manure, and soil [34], so conduit water was tested for the drugs quantitation after it filtered three times through a qualitative filter paper to remove the insoluble matters and preserved in high-quality clean plastic containers. 10.0 mL water samples were spiked with 1.0 µM standard solution of SPX and ORX and stored at −4 °C until analysis.

Dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction procedure (dMSPE) was processed through MMNPs as sorbents to extract SPX and ORX from real samples. Certain SPX and ORX aliquots of the as-obtained milk supernatants or natural water were completed to 20.0 mL with B.R. buffer (pH 4.0). 15.0 mg of MMNPs was added, and vortexed for 15 min to ensure full adsorption of SPX or ORX on the MMNPs exterior surface. After that, an external magnet was employed to separate MMNPs from the solution mixture, followed by decantation of the supernatant and elution of the analyte via 400 μL methanol by sonication for 5 min. The solution was then separated by placing a magnet and combined with B-CQDs in the following general procedure for subsequent fluorimetric assay (Scheme 1). The UV/Vis was used to ensure that all SPX and ORX were completely extracted from the MMNPs. The complete extraction was achieved after invisible UV detection of SPX or ORX in the eluate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for sparfloxacin and orbifloxacin detection
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 mL B-CQDs solution, 1.0 mL of B.R. buffer (pH. 6), and different amounts of SPX or ORX were added, and the mixture was diluted to 4.0 mL with DDW and mixed well. The fluorescence spectra were measured after 10 min at λex/λem of 370/433 nm with a slit width of 5 nm for all experiments.

The stock solution was obtained by dissolving 100.0 mg of analytical pure SPX and ORX in 1.0 L DDW to get an initial concentration of 100.0 mg/L. The stock solution was further diluted with DDW to get the desired concentrations. All batch adsorption experiments were carried out using a beaker (50.0 mL) in a vortex mixer apparatus, 30 mg of the prepared MMNPs was dispersed in 20 mL of 100 mg/L of SPX and ORX and was vortexed for 1 h at room temperature. In a time range of 10–100 min with 10 min intervals, ten samples were taken out and MEM concentration in supernatant was measured by the proposed fluorimetric method.

Adsorption capacity (q) of MMNPs was determined using the following mathematical expression [24]:

Since Cf and Ci are the concentrations of SPX and ORX in the eluent and the initial concentration of the cited drugs in the sample solution, respectively.

The recyclability of MMNPs was also conducted. After drug adsorption, the magnetic system was washed with methanol; MMNPs were separated by magnetic decantation as described above, dried and collected, washed thoroughly with DDW, and dried. The product was then reused for adsorption in the next adsorption cycle.

Fig. 1 displays TEM images and the size distribution of the B-CQDs. The TEM characterization obviously confirms that the B-CQDs are mono-dispersed nanoparticles with a narrow size distribution and average diameter of 12.4 nm, respectively.

The FTIR was applied to investigate the functional groups of the synthesized CQDs and B-CQDs ( Fig. 2). For CQDs, the absorption bands at 3411, 1693, 1622, 1097, and 1236 cm−1 are assigned to υ (‒OH), υ (C˭O), δ (C˭C), υ (C‒O‒C), and υ (‒C‒O) respectively [36]. The characteristic bands at 2952, 1424, 1031, and 796 cm−1 are attributed to υ (B‒OH), υ (B‒C), υ (B‒O), and υ (B‒O‒B) respectively, which confirms the doping of boron in the carbonaceous structure of the synthesized CQDs in compare to the undoped CQDs [37].

Fig. S1 shows the PXRD of the B-CQDs and undoped CQDs. It was found that the B-CQDs and undoped CQDs samples exhibit one broad at 2 θ = 23.7°, corresponding to the graphite phase (002) plane [27]. While weak diffraction peaks at 2 θ = 11.7° (001) and 42.3° (100) for B-CQDs and undoped CQDs of (001) and (100), respectively, may be attributed to derivatives generated from the hydro-pyrolysis procedure of glucose.

Paul Scherrer's formula (Eq. (3)) was used to calculate the average particle size for the [002] diffraction peak [38]:
(4)
L = K ∙
λ
β COS θ

Since λ is the X-ray wavelength, β is the line broadening at half the maximum intensity after subtracting the instrumental line broadening, in radians, θ is the corresponding diffraction angle (2θ = 23.7°) and K is the Scherrer’s constant which accounts for the shape of the particle. Having K = 0.94 and λ = 1.542, an L = 13.1 nm was obtained which is matching with TEM and DLS results. This average particle size is slightly larger than other reported [26,27], which may be attributed to the poor crystallinity of the synthesized B-CQDS in a turbostratic form [39]. It can be seen that the diffraction peaks of B-CQDs shifted slightly to left compared with the undoped CQDs which might indicate slight damage to the graphitic structure of carbon dots by boron [40]. Fig. S2 shows the EDS spectrum of B-CQDs displays three characteristic peaks at 0.19, 0.25, and 0.51 keV, which correlate with B (21.83 Atom. wt%), C (48.43 Atom. wt%), and O (29.74 Atom. wt%), respectively (Inset of the Fig. S2). And the B/O atomic ratio is obviously less than the B/O ratio of the precursors indicating a large fraction of O atoms goes lost during synthesis to initiate the oxidation of glucose in presence of borate to form B-CQDs which might be a crucial point in the modulation of physicochemical characters of B-CQDs [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of boron-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Regarding the UV–Vis spectroscopy, there are two distinct absorption peaks at 205 nm and 310 nm as shown in Fig. 3a. The first peak is attributed to π–π* electronic transition and the second peak is attributed to n–π* electronic transition and the integration of the functional surface [42]. A high electron density of unsaturated groups integrated with the doped boron and upgraded the charge density of B-CQDs, resulting in energy transfer to the excited π* state of the sp2 state of B-CQDs [39]. Moreover, B-CQDs has a characteristic emission peak at 433 nm upon exciting at 370 nm, proving that the B-CQDs (Fig. 3b). Excitation dependent emission is a typical property belonging to most CQDs, that is, the emission is tunable under excitation at different wavelengths. Fig. 3c showed red-shifted emission from 370 to 520 nm whenever excitation wavelengths were increased from 340 nm to 430 nm [43]. In quantitative fluorometric analysis, the photo-stability of the synthesized CQDs may be considered as the master criteria. Regarding this phenomenon, the emission property of B-CQDs was scanned under persistent 365 nm UV illuminations for two hours at 30 min intervals. Fig. 3d exhibits the good photo-stability of B-CQDs as the fluorescence signal remains fixed up to 120 min after UV illumination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield calculation of boron-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The single-point method was proceeded to calculate the Quantum yield (QY) of B-CQDs [44] employing Rhodamine B (QY = 0.31) standard solution as a reference according to the following equation:

where Q is fluorescence quantum yield, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, ɳ is the refractive index of solvent, and OD is the optical density (absorption). The subscript “st” refers to rhodamine B reference standard solution and “ x ” refers to B-CQDS solution. After calculation, the quantum yield was calculated and found to be 26.3% at λex = 370 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of mannitol functionalized magnetic nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: TEM image displays spherical monodispersed MMNPs with 17 nm average size. Comparing The TEM data of MNPs with mannitol ( Fig. 4a) and without mannitol incorporation (Fig. 4b) reveal the significance of surface passivation of MNPs with mannitol in the stability, smoothing, and well-distribution of the synthesized particles thanks to mannitol preventing MNPs aggregation. TEM micrograph shows that MMNPs are spherical in shape with a mean particle size of 17 nm. SEM micrograph shown in Fig. 4c corresponds to stacked MNPs, while the homogenous distribution of nanoparticles in Fig. 4d shows the importance of mannitol as a capping agent.

The FTIR spectrum of MNPs (Fig. S3a) shows the expected Fe‒O stretching absorption at 507 cm−1 which shifts to a high wavenumber of 548 cm−1 in MMNPs FT-IR spectrum (S3b). The bands at 2954 and 1134 in the MMNPs sample were indexed to stretching and bending vibrations (respectively) of ‒CH2 while the band at 1134 cm−1 was attributed to C‒OH stretching vibration. The band at 3512 cm−1 could be attributed to the ‒OH stretching vibration of H2O adsorbed by the MNPs sample. In contrast, the relatively narrowed band located at the same wavenumber in MMNPs was assigned to ‒OH of mannitol species. The relatively medium absorption peak at around 1653 cm−1 in MNPs assigned to the absorption of the OH bending signal of adsorbed water while it disappeared or may be obscured by ‒CH2 bending in MMNPs [25]. As a result of FTIR assignments, the mannitol groups are well distributed on the exterior surface of MNPs.

The PXRD measurements ( Fig. 5) show diffraction peaks of 31.54°, 35.26°, 42.76°, 53.68°, 57.64°, 62.56°, and 74.74° indexed as (220, 311, 400, 422, 511, 440, and 553) reflections which confirms the face-centered cubic (fcc) inverse spinel structure of Fe3O4 NPs [45]. The sharpness of PXRD reflections clearly shows that the synthesized Fe3O4 is highly crystalline. The average particle size was calculated using Scherrer's equation which previously mentioned in detail in Eq. (3); the average particle size was found to be 17.4 nm which is agreed with TEM results.

Elemental analysis of the magnetic product is shown in the inset of Fig. S4. In a pure Fe3O4 compound iron content is 70% [45], thus the product purity can be calculated using Eq. (5):
(6)

The product purity = 67.52/70 × 100 = 96.46%, indicating a high purity value can be obtained by virtue of the current synthesis procedure in comparison with others such as [46].
Fig. S5 shows the vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) where the mannitol modified MNPs exhibit characteristic shape, proving the super-paramagnetic feature of the MMNPs [36].

The N2 adsorption-desorption measurements were investigated in Fig. S6. The results prove that the isotherm can be categorized as type IV with H3 hysteresis loop according to IUPAC. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) specific surface area of MMNPs was found to be 195.24 m2 g−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the dispersive micro-solid-phase extraction method
ce:section.ce:para: MSPE efficiency is mainly subject to the following major factors: MNPs surface modifier, Amount of sorbent, sample volume, pH, shaking type, shaking time, eluting solvent and its volume, and extraction time. In this study, these were investigated and optimized to achieve the best extraction efficiency of ORX and SPX, using the prepared magnetic nanoparticles [25]. Optimization experiments were performed using standard aqueous solutions spiked with 50.0 nM ORX and SPX. The relative fluorescence intensity (RFI) was used as the experimental response to evaluate the influence of these factors on the extraction efficiency of dMSPE to the target analytes, and all the optimization experiments were conducted three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Type of magnetic nanoparticles surface modifier
ce:section.ce:para: The selection of the adsorbent material is considered to be the cornerstone of the extraction potential of an analyte. Four different MNPs of different surface modifications were tested for their efficiencies on SPX and ORX extraction: iron magnetic nanoparticles (Fe-MNPs), starch-coated magnetic nanoparticles (starch-MNPs), SDS coated magnetic nanoparticle (SDS-MNPs) and mannitol coated magnetic nanoparticle (MMNPs). As seen in Fig. S7a, mannitol magnetic nanoparticles (MMNPs) gave the highest value of RFI. These results may be a result of plenty of hydroxyl moieties enables mannitol to form considerable hydrogen-bonding with solvent (improve water dispersibility) and with drugs such as SPX and ORX that contain lone pair of electrons providing more cites/larger surface area for adsorption [25]. Based on these results, mannitol coated magnetic nanoparticles (MMNPs) were used as an excellent nanosorbent for the extraction of SPX and ORX from milk and water samples.

Regarding the amount of sorbent, five different amounts of MMNPs (5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 mg) were tested while the other experimental conditions were fixed. Fig. S7b shows that the highest RFI was obtained for the 15 mg amount, noting that the RFI markedly decreased for the 20 and 25 mg amounts. This may be due to the non-covalent interaction between nanoparticles which results in agglomeration and loss of available active surface area [47]. Thus, 15 mg MMNPs was chosen as the optimum amount and used in further experiments.

SPX and ORX are zwitterionic molecules containing two proton-binding sites, so the sample solution should be adjusted to a suitable pH to ensure efficient adsorption. In the current work, the pH values of the sample solutions were adjusted to 4.0, 7.0, and 10.0, the RFI of SPX and ORX at pH 4.0 and 7.0 were obviously higher than those at pH 10 (Fig. S7c). This may due to the piperazine groups of SPX and ORX were protonated at low pH, which might form an electrostatic attraction with the hydroxyl group on the MMNPs surface, and so a higher amount of ORX was extracted. On the other hand, with the increase of pH, the deprotonated piperazine, and oxo-anionic carboxyl in ORX may cause the electrostatic repulsion with ‒OH [48]. Thus, pH 4.0 was selected as the optimum extraction pH. The results confirm the critical role of the sorbent hydroxyl moiety in the extraction procedure of the cited analytes. Regarding pH < 4.0, It is noteworthy that the MNPs are soluble in a highly acidic medium [45] in addition to the high competition of the large amount of H+ with cationic SPX or ORX species leading to negligible or no absorption [49].

To further study the effect of pH variation and surface charge on the adsorption mechanism, the zeta-potential of MMNP at different pH values were measured. From zeta-potential measurements (Fig. S8), it has been observed that adsorption of mannitol onto the surface of MNPs results in highly negative surface charge and the isoelectric point (IOP) is not observed in measured pH range of 2.0–8.0 (IOP of bare MNPs is 5.9 as in other studies such as) [50]. The high negative zeta-potential values for the MMNP further proved the presence of negatively charged hydroxyl groups on the exterior of MNPs [51]. It is attractive to mention that the MNPs were stable in the studied pH range and no precipitation was looked out. This result indicates the nanoparticle stabilization assessed by steric repulsion [52]. The surface charge of the MMNPs at pH 4.0 (the pH at which the drug loading experiment was processed) was found to be negative (−22.6 mV) from zeta-potential measurements. An increase in surface charge from −22.6 to −10.3 mV is observed for the MMNP after drug loading in zeta-potential measurement, confirming that positively charged SPX and ORX molecules are bound to negatively charged MMNP through electrostatic attraction.

To study the effect of sample volumes on the extraction efficiency of ORX and SPX, a series of volumes of B.R. buffer, pH 4.0, spiked with 50 nM of SPX and ORX (10.0–50.0 mL) were examined. The results in Fig. S7d have demonstrated that the RFI increased with increasing sample volume from 10.0 mL to 20.0 mL and then gradually decreased due to loss of analytes from the adsorbent surface (low mass transfer rate) [24]. As a result, a 20.0 mL sample volume was selected for subsequent analysis.

Salt content in the aqueous samples may alter the ionic strength of the solution or affect the diffusion rate of the analyte from aqueous to solid phases. To study the salt effect on the extraction of SPX/ORX antibiotics, various amounts of NaCl (0–100 M) were added to the solution to adjust salinity. As shown in Fig. S7e, RFI for SPX and ORX sharply declined as the concentration of NaCl increased.

Obviously, the highest RFI signal was given when no NaCl was added. This may because that NaCl would weaken the static electric interaction between MNPs and mannitol, and diminish the stability of mannitol-MNPs for MSPME. Also, the addition of NaCl would diminish mass transfer by altering the Nernst diffusion layer [53], probably leading to the decreased diffusion rate of the analytes to the surface of the magnetic nanoparticles [54]. Thus, no NaCl was added in the next procedures.

Five different shaking types were used to investigate their effects on the RFI such as sonication, stirring, mechanical, vortex, and no shaking. As seen in Fig. S7f, vortex assisted solid-phase extraction gave the highest RFI with a relatively lowest RSD%. Moreover, vortex shaking for 15, 30, 45, and 60 min was applied to select the optimum mixing time. The experiments exhibit that the higher mixing periods than 15 min result in higher % RSD due to poor repeatability (Fig. S7g). So, 15 min was selected as the optimum contact time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Type and volume of eluting solvent
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, five polar solvents were tested for elution solvent optimization: methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), acetonitrile (ACN), ethyl acetate (CH3COOEt), and propanol (ProOH). As seen in Fig. S7h, the highest RFI was obtained for methanol. So, methanol was selected as an optimum eluent for further experiments. This may due to the sufficient polarity and protic nature of MeOH that has the ability to destroy hydrogen bonding between MMNPs and polar drugs SPX and ORX Furthermore, different volumes of methanol (100, 200, 300, 400, 500, and 600 μL) were tested for their elution potentials. According to the results in (Fig. S7i), 400 μL was selected as the optimum volume of methanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible fluorescence enhancement of boron-doped carbon dots by orbifloxacin and sparfloxacin
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence enhancement strategy could be assigned to hydrogen bonding and electron-donor-acceptor complex between B-CQDs and ORX/SPX. The presence of numerous carboxyl, hydroxyl, and trivalent boron moieties provides viability to conjugate with the cited analytes depending on the two featured, active, and strong adjacent hydrogen bonds between the hydroxyl/carboxyl of B-CQDs and two fluorine of ORX or fluorine/nitrogen of SPX ( Scheme 2). Adjacent strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds can form a center that links two neighboring molecules. The π-electron delocalization within intermolecular hydrogen-bonded systems has consequences for the stabilization parameters of the rings, so the inter-molecular hydrogen bonds were regarded as resonance-assisted systems [55]. In all cases, the hydrogen bond strength and amount of resonance within the hydrogen-bonded system is related to the types of interactions (O‒H.F and /or O‒H.N) and to the molecular symmetry [56].

Besides, the electron-accepting character of the trivalent boron that stabilized by carbon skeleton in B-CQDs and the electron-donating property of the cited ORX and SPX participate in the donor-π-acceptor system by charge transfer which may promote the conjugation of C˭C bonds [57]. Merging of the hydrogen bonding and electron-donor-acceptor complex effect can enhance the initiation of massive chromophores and fluorophores (Fig. S9).

Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes of B-CQDs in the presence and absence of SPX and ORX were measured to investigate the fluorescence behavior of B-CQDs towards the two drugs. As seen in Fig. 6, the fluorescence lifetime of the pristine B-CQDs reduced from 10.13 ns to 6.45 and 6.08 ns in presence of ORX and SPX, respectively. The results indicate that SPX and ORX increase the number of radiative transition centers, accelerate the radiative decay of fluorescent B-CQDs, and eventually enhance the fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental parameters for sparfloxacin and orbifloxacin detection using boron-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the solution pH on the fluorescence sensitization of B-CQDs in the existence of the cited analytes is shown in Fig. S10a. By increasing pH from 2.0 to 5.0, RFI increases. Then, an additional pH increment from 5.0 to 7.0 results in negligible changes in the fluorescence intensity of the B-CQDs, whilst an additional increment in pH from 7.0 to 10.0, generates a successive lowering intensity.

The found results suggest that hydroxyl, carboxyl, and boronyl groups on the surface of B-CQDs may be protonated at low pH which will lead to aggregation of B-CQDs whereas in alkaline solution, neutralization reaction takes place and only little reactive sites were available for the proposed reaction [58]. As a consequence, we had chosen the B.R. buffer (pH 6.0) for the subsequent analysis.

The influence of B-CQDs volume on the fluorescence sensitization is presented in Fig. S10b. The fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the volume increment up to 0.4 mL, overpassing this volume, the fluorescence intensity hadn't affected. Thus, 0.5 mL was selected as the optimum volume for further study.

The effect of standing time on the fluorescence enhancement efficiency is shown in Fig. S10c. The fluorescence intensity appeared low and gradually increased until attained a plateau after ten minutes. The fluorescence intensity had not affected by overpassing 10 min. So, 10 min was selected as the optimal standing time.

The calibration plots were constructed by comparing 12 and 14 concentration points of ORX and SPX respectively to blank samples ( Fig. 7). Regression analysis was assessed employing that the ΔϜ value (ΔϜ = F − F0, as F and F0 are the RFI values in the presence and absence of analyte, respectively). Linearity ranges, determination coefficients (R2), limits of detection (LODs), limits of quantification (LOQs), enrichment factor (EF) and ER%, for the detection of the two cited FQs were calculated at the selected conditions. Intra and inter-day validations were studied to estimate the precision of the proposed methodology by applying the general procedure on samples of 10.0, 50.0, 100.0 nM concentrations for each drug ( Table 1).

The LODs and LOQs were calculated according to the following equations:
(7)
LOD = 3.3 ∙
σ
S
(8)
LOQ = 10
.

S
where σ is the standard deviation of the regression line, and s is the slope. The analytical performance proved that the proposed method has good reproducibility and potent viability for use in the quantitation of ORX and SPX at trace levels.

To investigate the selectivity of the proposed method, diverse FQs (including CPX, NRX, OFX, LVX, LMX, PFX, ENX, MOX, and FLQ) (Fig. S11) and other possible biological interferents (sucrose, urea, glycine, cysteine, Ca2+ and ascorbic acid) were subjected to the general procedure at the optimum conditions. The samples contained a fixed amount of interferents (1.0 µM), and their effect was determined using the proposed method in comparison to 50.0 nM ORX and SPX. As shown in Fig. 8, only ORX and SPX can enhance the fluorescence intensity while 1.0 µM of a wide range of FQs, and biological species didn't produce notable fluorescence responses. The suggested reason for high selectivity is attributed to the unique chemical structure of ORX and SPX; especially the presence of two adjacent strong hydrogen bonds that participate in resonated conjugation systems in addition to the electron-donor-acceptor complex between the quinolone ring of the cited analytes and B-CQDs. There is slight interference by means of 1.0 µM CPX or NRX, which may be due to hydrogen-bonding arises between the ‒OH of B-CQDs and the unsubstituted piperazine moiety unlike the other FQs containing substituted piperazine moiety as the substitution is unfavorable in hydrogen-bonding formation [59]. Without MSPE, the method did not exhibit high selectivity towards ORX and SPX in the presence of other FLQs especially CPX and NRX. The lack of selectivity overcame by MSPE, which was based on strong hydrogen bonding and electrostatic binding between MMNPs and the cited ORX and SPX.

To investigate the application of the proposed method in the real samples, the proposed method was applied to ORX and SPX detection in milk and water samples after they were prepared as previously described. The good recoveries % of SPX and ORX in milk and water prove that the MMNPs based fluorimetric method is efficiently applied for analysis of SPX and ORX with high accuracy with minimal interferences ( Table 2). Furthermore, on the basis of average recoveries and RSDs of Table 2, we can deduce that the analytical assay was obviously better than that processed without MSPE, proving that MSPE has a distinct role.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the proposed method with other methods
ce:section.ce:para: Comparing the results in the current method with other reported ones ( Table 3), it can be seen that B-CQDs can serve as a probe for the detection of ORX and SPX in a low concentration, and low %RSD, indicating higher sensitivity and reliability of the proposed fluorimetric approach for detection of the cited analytes.

The adsorption characterization of SPX and ORX were investigated on MMNPs and the results in Fig. 9 refer to the adsorption increased, at start, with time and reached a plateau after 15 min depending on the mass resistance transfer. The maximum adsorption capacity was 38.5 and 39.7 mg/g after 15 min for SPX and ORX respectively, confirming that short time and high adsorption capacity will be attained by the proposed adsorption procedure.

To investigate the adsorption kinetic, pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics models were adopted to evaluate the adsorption mechanism of SPX and ORX on MMNPs and the obtained data are summarized in Table S1. The pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics models are expressed in Eqs. (9) and (10), respectively.

where qe (mg/g) and qt (mg/g) are the adsorption capacity at equilibrium and at time t, respectively; k1 (min−1) is the pseudo-first-order rate constant; and k2 (g/(mg min)) is the pseudo-second-order rate constant, respectively.

The adsorption kinetics of MMNPs toward SPX and ORX are presented in Fig. S11. As shown in Fig. S12, the plot does not fit well with the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (R2 < 0.9 for the two cited drugs). In contrast and according to higher R2 value, the pseudo-second-order rate equations for SPX and ORX loading onto MMNPs have a better fit; since SPX or ORX diffuse from the liquid phase to a liquid–solid interface; move from a liquid–solid interface to solid surfaces; and finally, the drug molecules load onto the exterior of MMNPs [66], Related parameters are given in Table S1.

Langmuir model (LM) and Freundlich model (FM) were used to determine the equilibrium relationship between MMNPs and SPX and ORX molecules Eqs. (11) and (12). As observed in Table S2 and Fig. S13, the LM model gives better R2 than the Freundlich model and so can better describe the binding process, indicating that adsorption of the cited drugs takes place at specific active sites onto MMNPs and has found successful application for many adsorption processes of monolayer [24].

The linear forms of LM and FM are described as formulas (11) and (12), respectively;

Since kL and kF are Langmuir and Freundlich constants, respectively, n is the reciprocal of reaction, Ce (mg/L) is the equilibrium concentration of SPX and ORX, qmax (mg/g) and qe (mg/g) is maximum adsorption capacity, and the amount of adsorbed drugs per unit mass of adsorbent at equilibrium, respectively.

Recycling experiments were achieved, the MMNPs were consecutively reused six times for adsorption of 50.0 nM of SPX and ORX without any noticeable loss of its adsorbing activity ( Fig. 10) and no regeneration process is required after each reaction [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The lifetime stability of the sorbent
ce:section.ce:para: MMNPs show good stability in water (up to one month) and retain their superior magnetic properties up to the tested period of one week (Fig. S14). Elemental analysis of the magnetic product is shown in Table S3, indicating no noticeable loss of its iron content. The stability could be due to the mannitol passivation on the surface of nanoparticles, which protects the inner iron oxide nanoparticles against storage deterioration.

In conclusion, novel adapted routes for the preparation of boron-doped carbon dots (B-CQDs) and mannitol magnetic nanoparticles (MMNPs) were presented. B-CQDs as an innovative type of luminophore and MMNPs as an innovative nanosorbent were combined for interferences free determination of orbifloxacin (ORX) and sparfloxacin (SPX) for the first time. The interaction is based on hydrogen bond formation and electron-donor-acceptor complex between B-CQDs and ORX/SPX after preconcentration with mannitol modified magnetic nanoparticles. As a further matter, it is attractive that the proposed strategy has been utilized as an efficient method for the quantitation of ORX and SPX with high selectivity and sensitivity, and perfect applicability in real milk and natural water samples with satisfactory results. The main drawback of the extracting/sensing method is the inability to discriminate between SPX and ORX if they are present together.
